failure_modes.text: Law-of-small-numbers bias makes tiny samples look more representative than they really are.
failure_modes.source_quote: The Law of Small Numbers Bias
failure_modes.mitigation: Flag small-sample conclusions explicitly: tiny samples are far more volatile than intuition suggests.

failure_modes.text: Exaggerated faith in scraps can turn a few observations into hasty confident judgment.
failure_modes.mitigation: Require a stated sample size and confidence interval before treating any observation as a reliable pattern.

failure_modes.text: Large-N logic can mislead when the sample is biased or drawn from the wrong population.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger When Large-N Logic Is Applied to the Wrong Population
failure_modes.mitigation: Check whether the sample is drawn from the relevant population; large-N logic misleads when the sample is biased or mismatched.

premortem_questions.text: Is the worst case bad enough?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Is the worst case bad enough?

premortem_questions.text: Are we letting a vivid story stand in for the population-level evidence?
premortem_questions.source_quote: The Availability Heuristic encourages reliance on a small, biased sample of experiences

premortem_questions.text: Have we disaggregated the conjunctive plan enough to see where the overall probability actually collapses?

heuristics.text: Use probabilistic thinking so fair bets and risk estimates reflect frequencies instead of vivid anecdotes.
Apply reference class forecasting before arguing that this case is uniquely special.
Use simulations to inspect the full distribution instead of anchoring only on the mean outcome.

curation_notes.summary: Focused on anti-small-sample discipline, distribution awareness, and reference-class correction rather than broad statistics instruction.
donor_drops: Did not preserve donor textbook-style exposition because the raw source already gives stronger intervention chunks around anecdotes, wrong populations, and tail-risk discipline.
open_questions: The second premortem question is normalized from the availability conflict because the source is stronger on the risk than on a direct quoted question.

allies: The raw allies section is strong on bayesian thinking, systems emergence, and expertise-through-quantity for later relation preservation.
antagonists: The raw antagonists section includes representativeness and availability, but only the cleanest canonical ids should survive later relation curation.
structured_tensions: The source is tension-real around base-rate neglect and vivid-story distortion even though it lacks a dedicated structured tension block.
